speaker: interviewer
text: --Gentile by the name of Irene Gut-Opdyke. And this is tape 8. Irene, you were telling me a few minutes ago that you are outraged about the fact that so little is said about the Righteous Gentiles in Poland. What do you want to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I want to say that it hurts me sometimes. And I am very close-- Jewish people were like my family because I was with them through the whole, horrible time. But now that I am speaking for [? UDA, ?] raising funds for Israel, which is very close to my heart. Israel has to be strong. It's no question about. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And to hear that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Polish concentration camps, Polish death camps-- there were not Polish. They were German-built concentration camps in Poland. Poland was not free country. She was forced. They forced them to work, you know-- to saying silly things like Polish lumber builded the Auschwitz. Polish workers builded the concentration camps. Well, German did not bring the lumber from Germany. And Polish worker, yes, they were slaves. Like others, they were forced to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would not say there were not people that would sell their own mother for vodka or food. But you know that we giving the Jewish children-- see, I am speaking from one end of United States to other. And in the beginning, there was so much hate. When I finish, they ask me, there was anyone that helped?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, this is not right. Somehow is-- somebody is guilty. And we're doing to our children-- to the second generation and now third generation-- we're doing the same that was done to the Jewish people. Building-- I am trying-- I am not putting hate to anyone. I am trying to unite people together. And the-- I did have many meetings now. When I raised funds for Israel, which is very close to my heart, many times people say, oh, Mrs. Opdyke, my grandmother, or my grandfather, or somebody-- and that many people say, was hidden here, was saved by Polish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when I ask them, did you report it? No. I ask why not? Well, it's so late. We don't know if they're alive, or this and that. You know, Hitler wanted Poland. He brought it-- he brought it from all over different countries people to the crematorias. What the Polish people could do? I mean, you know? Many-- three million of Polish people died, too. They could not do anything. What is your message to your grandchildren, to your own grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: This what my mother give it to me. I am-- in any schools I speak, I tell them, you are the future. The hate doesn't accomplish anything. You have to be responsible. And to-- you're lucky. You are our children from all nationalities. You are lucky. Just look at each other. Learn from each other. This what is all about. Hate ruin you, your family. And hate ruin the country. You have to. And I believe in you-- you, the children from the whole world. You unite together, you will be able to build better future. And you children from high school and college, soon you be grown-up. You assume role in your communities. When you marry, do not teach your children hate. Children are not born evil. You are made evil. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's what I am speaking.

speaker: interviewer
text: Irene, before I thank you for speaking to us and, and opening your heart to us, and before I thank you for what you have done for us, for my people, I would like to speak a moment about your sisters who are visiting you here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you think maybe they would come and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I will call them. Or maybe if you can call them.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like very much. You know, they are wonderful ladies. If I would know-- knew them before I write the book, there would be many things I've forgotten that they remembered. And they were persecuted. They worked hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Irene, will you introduce your sisters to us and then translate my questions to them and their answers to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: I will do that, yes. This is my sister, [? Bronia, ?] Bronislawa. And this is the little one, the youngest one, Wladislawa. They are both the younger sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: First of all, ask them, how do they like the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: She loves it very much because she did take me in care.

speaker: interviewee
text: She even wroted a, a, you know, song about--

speaker: interviewer
text: She's the one that writes the poem?

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your other sister? What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she is saying that I love America. And naturally, when I did see her, you know, I could not believe it. Because when I left, she was very little. She was, like, I'm her mama now, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ask [? Bronia, ?] is she the oldest? Is, is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you ask her, how does she feel about your work today, what you're doing today?

speaker: interviewee
text: She is very proud. She's very proud. And they are really, both of them proud, and proud that I'm traveling, speaking to children, that I'm trying to bring back better, better condition. The hate doesn't do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she write any poems about--

speaker: interviewee
text: America? She wrote it about me. And that's a beautiful-- but it's in Polish, naturally, not translated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we-- can she maybe just say one paragraph, and you will translate it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: She has that downstairs to read it. She cannot remember from that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Irene, your sister, [? Bronia, ?] she is a poet, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you ask her to read a small part of one of her poems and translate it to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it says, my dear sister, my dear sister, [CRYING] though you are short and nobody's-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] they thinking about you. Nobody building a big--

speaker: interviewee
text: --monuments. But in our hearts, you are the biggest one. And you are our heroine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, would you tell her that you are our heroine also, and that you are in our heart much bigger than your size?

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- I am very, very happy about. They really love me very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she says, is very honored-- is very honored, you know. And when I go traveling and speaking, they-- I just pray to God that I can keep them longer. I am getting old. And I need-- see, when [? Wladia ?] came about a year ago, just before that, I needed surgery. And it would be not for her, I don't know how I would survive. Because the Medicare did not want to pay for my surgery. They said, it is cosmetic. I did have cancer 15 years ago. They put an implant. And the implant break. And finally, they made it-- other-- little organization did the surgery, what was done in a doctor's office. Four hours after surgery, they brought me home with the drainages hanging. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it would be not for my sister-- I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who finally paid for it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they did-- they assigned me to a little MaxiCare that paid $5 doctor-- $7. That's all it is. But I work all my life of 1949. Second day in the United States, I work. I never took out unemployment. But now I'm old. And there is no help for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also, I would like to have a sister with me. But they allowed only a visa-- visitor's visa for six months. That's all. And I am 72 years old. And for sisters, it's like 12 years. Well, would I bring them if I am in the 80s? I don't know if I will live that long.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Well, let's hope. It's not right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's hope that you will find a way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And please tell them that I'm so grateful that they were willing to speak to us and--

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING POLISH] This is my heroine, that she help me to survive. And this one, you know, come now. And she's-- this is our clown, our-- she makes us laugh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Irene, again, I want to thank you and your sisters, but especially, thank you as a survivor for what you have done for my people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. And if you want to see the--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to show some of your pictures and some of-- some of your mementos-- righteous--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, thank you. Thank you so much. This was really wonderful to have you here in my home. You are wonderful-- very warm person.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the gentleman there, he is working very hard. And he's very-- just so.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to show some pictures now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is my parents, Maria Gut-- from home, Rembish. And this is my father, Wladislaw Gut.

speaker: interviewee
text: They are also my parents, Maria and Wladislaw Gut. And from the left, my youngest sister, Wladia. Then next is my second sister, Janina.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is their last name? Please say their last name, also.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the married name. So, Wladia [NON-ENGLISH], Janina Lorek, Maria [? Kraus, ?] Irene Opdyke, and Bronislawa Goraj.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1992. But at that time, some of them were married. Some were not married yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my sisters. The first one from the left is Wladislawa Gut, Irene Gut, Maria Gut, Bronislawa Gut, and Janina Lorek.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is done in Radom also. But that was earlier. It was 1939. This is my sister, Janina-- Janina Gut. And that's Irene Gut, also. And it is during the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is during the war when I did see killing. That is by Lwow. There were many people killed there, in the-- in that forest. And days later, I was able to make a picture. This stone, that's only one thing that marked the lives of many people-- was the shallow grave. And it was just put the dirt back. And they did not bother even to put names or any sign.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in Lwow. And that was in 1942.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in Radom before we left to, to Ukraine. I am on the farthest right with some girls that served in the diner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the, the-- some of those girls next to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were Polish. But there, on the left side, then without the, the shirt, there are some Jewish girls, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Major Edward Rugamer. And this is done in 1943 in the Ukraine in Tarnopol.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us who he was?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the head of the German organization that supplied ammunition, guns, and transportation for the German front. This is picture made in Cracow in 1945. That was just after the Liberation. And there was people that I was hiding in the German major villa. This is, from left, [NON-ENGLISH]. There is Franka Zylberman, Henry Weinbaum. This is Steiner and Paola, Paola Marx, Irene. And this is Mr. [? Rosen, ?] that was not hidden by me. But he was a friend. And he posed also. This is also right after the war in 1945. And this is Mr. Moises Weiss and his wife. In the last photo, those people, Mr. and Mrs. Weiss, they were not-- they were helped by me, but in the forest. And this one here is Helen, Helen Weinbaum. And the second man, I don't know. He was a friend in the army. Then the third one is, naturally, me, and then Henry Weinbaum.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was done in Cracow right after the war. And naturally, Henry Weinbaum was hidden by me. But Helen was, was his fiance that was very, very helpful, another a Christian girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is also in Cracow. From the left, this is Irene. That's me. And in the new suit, that's Heshu, who I was hiding. He was the, the man-- the Schneider that made for me the suit. And I was wearing it with pride. And next to him is Paola Marx, his wife. And they are the people that I met in 1993. Not really met them-- speak to them, but find about them. I am still not-- In touch with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am in touch. But I did not really met them personally. She is sick now.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, what year was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- somebody took the picture in Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when, what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was not-- also right after the war in '95, before I went searching. This is both Leizer Heller and Ida Heller and their little Roman. This is the boy whose life begun in German major villa. This picture I received when I met Hellers in Germany. And at that time, because I did not have the picture, they gave me a, a picture of them when they just immigrated to Germany, to Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- what year was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was probably, oh, he was about three, four years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know when was done, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Munich, Germany. And he was at that time about four years old. He was born 1944, so that would be about 1948 or something like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Roman Heller. He is now big. He grew up. And I was invited now for his son's bar mitzvah. And that was in 1984.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is [? Frances ?] [? Greenberg, ?] former Franka Zylberman. We reunited in 1992 in, in Los-- in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Yes, Yes, this is Israel. We're planting the tree. There is Rabbi Chaim Asa, who is in Jerusalem. And I am planting the tree for the Righteous Gentiles. And there is a, a man from from the Yad Vashem and also a secretary from the Yad Vashem. What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is happy Irene Gut in America, just before I married. It was in 1950, really, 1950, yes. This is my wedding pictures. My husband, William K. Opdyke-- we were just married. Happy, happy, happy couple.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was in 1955. And it was in San Francisco in a church.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband already not well. He was sick. This is 1990, 1990. And my husband did have Alzheimer's.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was 1992 taken to the home for the aged in Reseda--

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did he pass away?

speaker: interviewer
text: And when, when did he pass on?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was dead in 1982. And he was dead five years. Three years ago, he passed away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was taken in Yorba Linda here.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is reunited with my sisters. And this was in 1984.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Cracow, we did the picture. From the left, I am sitting. Then there is the youngest, [? Wladia. ?] And then, in the middle is [? Bronia, ?] and sitting on a-- Janina. On the right side is standing Maria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you-- would you repeat the names with their last names today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Irene Opdyke, Wladia [NON-ENGLISH], Bronislawa Goraj, Maria Kraus, and Janina Lorek.

speaker: interviewee
text: We are all-- and I've never been to any-- to the weddings.
